title: Keep things simple — pitching, presence, and the legacy question
html:
TL;DR

A scratchpad from late high-school / early-college Yahir, alternating between pitch-deck question prompts (disruption, emerging vs disruptive, "how do y'all look into the future") and a parallel column of meaning-of-life questions: legacy, mortality, who to surround yourself with. He's already practicing the muscle of the elevator pitch and articulating fear of not doing as the dominant fear. Closes with a contact list — yebw director, frank helle, "Albert from Simlock."

Key quotes

"Fear of not doing is greater than fear of doing it."

"It is a two way road — investors and invented. Dating."

"Have an elevator pitch for your self."

"Opportunity are anywhere and everywhere."

Why it matters in the graph

This is the earliest fingerprint of the founder-Yahir voice: the legacy question template, the pitch-as-dating analogy, and a contact-keeping discipline that prefigures the rankett meetup-prep era. john maxwell is the first named external influence ("you've got to have people in your corner").

Open questions

  • Who is "Albert from Simlock"? — orphan contact name worth surfacing if it recurs
  • Did the YEBW pitch deck survive anywhere in raw/?
  • The "two-way road / investors and invented" line — has Yahir restated this in any rankett notes?

Wikilinks

disruption · elevator pitch · fear of not doing · legacy question · yebw · john maxwell · frank helle · rankett · Memory
